# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Movies that heavily feature a synthisizer in the soundtrack

**Score:** 30
**Body:** My brothers and I pick a theme every month and try and watch as many movies based around that theme. My older brother picked the theme of 'Forgive ne father, for I have Sinthed". And he explained that it's movies that really focus on that synthesizer sound.

This is not my area of expertise so help me out! 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Tons of 80's movies. Giorgio Moroder, Vangelis and Tangerine **Dream** had a strangle hold on the movie soundtrack synth scene.

Blade Runner  
Neverending Story  
Risky Business  
Scarface  
Midnight Express  
Flash Dance  
DC Cab  
Beverly Hills Cop 1&2  
Rocky 4  
Tron (All three)  
Halloween 1&2  
Terminator  
Fire Starter  
Cat's Eye  
Cat People
  - Chariots of Fire
2. John Carpenter usually does the soundtrack of his movies and it's usually synth heavy afaik. 

Tron Legacy is a must for that theme also.
3. Anything scored by Wendy Carlos
  - My fav is The Funeral of Queen Mary which starts the intro to A Clockwork Orange. 
4. Drive. Nicolas Winding Refn films often have a lot of synths. Any soundtrack by Cliff Martinez or Johnny Jewel will do the trick too. 

Liquid Sky has a very cool and kooky synth soundtrack. 

Near Dark has a great soundtrack by Tangerine Dream.

Good Time has a great soundtrack by Oneohtrix Point Never.
5. Tron Legacy 100%
6. A Clockwork Orange
7. Tangerine Dream have been on soundtracks for a number of movies. 

Thief directed by Michael Mann.  

Risky Business was Tom Cruise’s breakout movie and has great synth music during a sexy train scene. 

Sorcerer (1977) is considered an under recognized masterpiece. Directed by William Friedkin who also directed The Exorcist and The French Connection.
8. It follows

## 2. Beautiful Fuckery

**Score:** 19
**Body:** I need movies that fit the vibe of:

The Substance

Requiem for a Dream

The Menu

Uncut Gems

Bring Her Back

Each of these movies are absolutely gorgeous, but they leave me unsettled in a very specific way. The atmospheres of each of these movies are phenomenal. Each movie really has its own unique flavor.

To be clear, I’m not looking to be unsettled more than anything, I want them to be actually great movies as well.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Honorable Mention: Midsommar and Hereditary 
2. Haven’t seen Beau is Afraid yet.
3. Infinity Pool 
4. Alejandro Jodorowsky films 
5. The Lobster, Killing of a Sacred Deer
6. I feel like Bugonia fits this category! Just watched it and LOVED it.
  - That was fun! It finally made me give Jesse Plemons the respect he deserves (even if the academy hasn’t)
7. It Follows

Possessor

Titane
8. So many good recs here already. Adding to those that mentioned The Lobster, Killing of a Sacred Deer, Infinity Pool, Midsommar, etc:

Triangle

Coherence

The Endless

Melancholia

Under the Skin

The Coffee Table

Antichrist

Funny Games (original German version is considered by some to be better than the US remake, but the US remake is almost shot-for-shot the same)

Speak No Evil (2022 Danish version is far superior to the 2024 American version, but both are good)

Martyrs (French version; this one will leave you feeling unsettled for sure so proceed with caution, but if you enjoyed Bring Her Back then this is only a small stretch)
9. Mother!

Saltburn

## 3. What are some movies where people lose a person and find a doppelganger?

**Score:** 15
**Body:** Looking for movies where someone loses a loved one and later meets a person who looks exactly like them. 

Doppelgangers, doubles, uncanny replacements, or reincarnation vibes all count.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Starman with Jeff Bridges

Sommersby with Richard Gere
  - Love starman!
2. Beerfest 
  - **"I'm Landfill's twin brother, Gil. I feel like I know you guys so well already, so we won't have that awkward get to know you phase. In fact, you can just call me Landfill."** 
3. Solaris (the original or the Soderbergh one) fits this perfectly. It’s more about the psychological toll of them coming back, though.


4. The Return of Martin Guerre (1982) —> this is the original story that Sommersby was based off of

Vertigo (1958)

Dead Ringer (1964) —> the characters are twins, but there are some elements of your request

Obsession (1976)

Edit: typo
  - Vertigo would be a great example of this 
5. I'm actually in before someone mentions Coherence? Wild! 
6. Bram Stoker's Dracula (1992)
7. The Addams Family - Uncle Fester

## 4. Movies that don’t make you feel lonely?

**Score:** 17
**Body:** Recently broke up with a friend. I need something that doesn’t make me feel alone. Something wholesome. Uplifting. Warm. I like romance movies, but I don’t want any hallmarks or anything that’s gonna make me feel worse about myself. I should be sleeping, so i wouldn’t mind something mellow to put me to sleep. Nothing too loud or headache-worthy.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Napoleon Dynamite 
2. The Big Lebowski 

My cure all when I'm in a bad mood movie 
3. zoolander! my all time favorite 
4. The Intern (2015)
5. Under the Tuscan Sun.

It will be like a warm hug, and you will forever appreciate ladybugs… 
6. Good boys 

Book smart

Bridesmaids

All hilarious
7. Im liking a lot of the older films, anything before the 2000s. Just looking up the suggestions so far, a lot looks intriguing.
8. in no particular order:

Song Sung Blue, One Chance, CODA

\*Introspective\*: Life is Beautiful , Pursuit of Happinness, The Secret Life of Walter Mitty, Lost in Translation, Being John Malkovich, The Truman Show, Ground Hog Day
9. Amelie(2001)
I had to watch with subtitles though as english dubbed version was not available.
10. Chef 💛

Marcel the Shell with Shoes on 

Anything from the Paddington Franchise 🇬🇧

 in the rom com sect of Adam Sandler movie verse- The Wedding Singer, and 50 first dates are all time favorites. 

Big Fat Greek Wedding (older) or Holidate (more recent) are also good low stakes, non friendship heavy, warm romance options. 


If you’re feeling out there, I think this would really fill a loneliness void 🥹🩷- Ryan gosling’s movie Lars and the Real Girl. it’s easy to assume bad things about this one from the cover or immediate reaction to the premise. Don’t! it is truly a wholesome, community affirming, weirdly empowering, also just weird, quirky, very human little love movie. 


** And my own choice tonight wanting similar vibes : Won’t You Be my Neighbor, the tom hanks movie about Mr. Fred Rogers and his unexpected friendship with a journalist who was expecting to dig up dirt on him in their time together. 



Other options I remembered as I typed that ^ 🤣
Julie and Julia , Mamma Mia , The Mighty Ducks 
If

## 5. Watch Babylon (1980)!!

**Score:** 13
**Body:** A total masterpiece that will blow you away. Idris Elba amongst others named it possibly the greatest British movie ever and I couldnt agree more. If you liked La Haine you'll love this. 

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Amazing film. 
2. Such a great film. What a sound track too.
3. I love this movie so much.
4. Ok I liked La Haine I'll give it a try.
5. La haine is in my top 4, and I’ve not heard of this. Thanks for the suggestion!
  - Let me know if you ever watch it cause it’s such a raw and powerful movie, and since you have la haine in your top 4 just like me I think you’ll love it 
    - I definitely will! 

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. What are your thoughts on Timothy Olyphant?

**Score:** 1404

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The chaotic energy he brought to Santa Clarita Diet was unmistakable. Fuck Netflix for canceling that show.

Edit: how can I forget his sushi chef cameo in the tv show The League? https://youtu.be/zUtkJ8IZ5y4?si=y9MZrZtB1GZPrUQx
  - That show was so good and he was outstanding in it. What a bummer. 
2. Raylan Givens is one of the coolest characters in the history of TV.
  - He and Walton Goggins in that show are top-tier.
    - They dug coal together.
  - 100% agree. Also Walton Goggins' Boyd Crowder is  the perfect 'sparring partner' to Raylan in that show. 
3. Seriously underrated, particularly in regards to his comedic timing, and I absolutely love his Conan interviews. 
4. an actor who should be more famous than he is. 
5. He was excellent in Deadwood
6. We LOVE him. Ever since Go

## 2. The Twilight Effect

**Score:** 633

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Nobody will ever convince me that the middle photo isn't actually Paul Bettany. 

Thanks for my first award!
  - Pattinson tried to convince Jennifer Connelly he was Bettany.
2. The best parts of Tenet were when he was on screen.
  - Gotta agree with you on that 
3. https://preview.redd.it/hwls1jgjmp0h1.png?width=960&format=png&auto=webp&s=126bf1d447f114ce704a31ed8975d8b9e9f834c1
  - That's always been such a gut-wrenching scene to me, to be honest. The reaction of his father just went straight to my heart.
    - My boy!!!!
4. The Twilight Effect is real Robert Pattinson completely reinvented himself into one of the most versatile and respected actors working today.


5. best actor of this generation
6. Yer a wizard, Cedric

## 3. In honor of his birthday what is your favorite Emilio Estevez performance?

**Score:** 244

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Young Guns
  - https://preview.redd.it/llmx9olwcr0h1.jpeg?width=600&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=27b7d88cad413505821ac2d198fb1ed2cde55455
  - Agreed.  Men at Work was such fun too!
2. Loaded Weapon 1
3. Repo Man. Wild ass movie
4. The breakfast club or The mighty ducks 
  - Mighty Ducks for sure but I'm from Minnesota so I'm biased.

Also that movie was the catalyst to make a new NHL franchise with a badass logo so there's that.
5. Top 3 in no particular order:

Judgement Night

Men At Work

The Breakfast Club
6. He and Richard Dreyfuss were great together in Stakeout. 
7. Men at Work. He was great in Young Guns, too. 

## 4. What's your top 3 Jake Gyllenhaal movies?

**Score:** 192
**Body:** Me : Night Crawler, Prisoners, enemy 

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Donnie,nightcrawler,prisoners
  - This is the way, with Zodiac a close 4th place.
2. **Prince of Persia, Bubble Boy, & Day After Tomorrow**, obviously
  - You want $500 dollar???
3. Night Crawler, End of Watch, October Sky
4. ![gif](giphy|bRvrsx60cNdeM)

  
Brokeback Mountain 1️⃣
  - It’s my favorite but my heart has to be in a place to watch it. 

So fucking good
5. Nightcrawler, Donnie Darko and Prisoners.
6. October Sky
7. End of Watch; Nightcrawler; Donnie Darko

In that order. End of Watch made me cry.

## 5. What’s a movie that frequently gets called “overrated” but that you think actually deserved all the hype/praise that it got?

**Score:** 143
**Body:** When it comes to fear, Hereditary just struck such a cord with me that no other movie really has before. I mean it when I say that this movie really, really got to me. Nowadays years after it’s release people tend to call it overrated and that say its just a mid tier horror movie. I feel like I’m usually the one to agree when movies are called overrated but I can’t with Hereditary, it’s truly a horror masterpiece IMO. 

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Hereditary was the most unnerved ive been with a horror movie for a looong time.  Especially for movies that actually focus on the horror part rather than just relying on gore scenes to unnerve people.  

I cant remember any before it hitting me so hard.
  - The way Toni captured grief was fucking incredible. It was so easy to buy into the metaphorical horror and the actual horror. And those last fifteen minutes were so unprecedented and crazy at the time it came out.
  - I’d just recently watch Tje VVitch when Hereditary came out, and Hereditary played out very similarly, actually.  It kind of proved that if you repackage The VVitch for a modern audience, it turns out it’s really fricken good.

Not to detract from Hereditary; it did some unique stuff and is a fantastic film in its own right, just saying The VVitch came out before and did a similar job of being a great horror film without leaning on the gimmicks you described.
2. Everything Everywhere All At Once (2022). Film bros will tell you it's overrated and sloppy, but I found it a genuinely moving story about finding what matters to you in the face of a chaotic, indifferent universe.
  - Got that Best Picture backlash. 
    - There's a lot of people whose entire relationship with media is trying to be too good for it.
3. Fight Club
4. More recently, it was Project Hail Mary.

I was expecting… actually idk what I was expecting.  Some kind of Interstellar knock off, I guess?  Instead I got something completely unique and, dare I say, heartwarming and optimistic.  It was a refreshing take on modern sci-fi and I’m so glad I got to see it in theaters.
5. Of the last decade, quite a few come to mind when it comes to hype and it being deserved. 

Top Gun: Maverick 

Hereditary 

Weapons 

Sinners 

Logan 

Nightcrawler 

The Batman 

Joker 


6. who calles hereditary over rated?

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. The Tree of Life (2011) is the greatest film I've ever seen and I couldn't tell you why

**Score:** 152
**Body:** just like the title says, it's the greatest film I've ever seen but for life of me I couldn't tell you why, it's almost like all the words escape me and I have only the "Pathos" that's left. 

after re-watching the film for 2nd time and now with director's cut, I can convincingly say this is the most gorgeous, thought-provoking and transcendental cinema that has ever graced the screen and the crazy thing is this film just simply goes beyond conventional boundaries of the art, it doesn't fall into any criteria when it comes to rating or even reviewing movie and therefore it's something you can only experience, not decipher and dissect it like we do with other movies, I feel like Malick intended it this way.

The Tree of Life is as abstract and directionless as movie can get but with incredible visual story-telling skills of Malick, the way he sets up everything in his films, he can have minimal dialogue in the movie ( although the most spoken words in his films are monologues tbh )  and create the impact so huge that you would think it wouldn't be possible without some kind of build-up and dialogue heavy scenes where each character is fleshed out, which is usually the case and the characteristic of the great film, but no, Malick does everything so uniquely and he is simply operating from his own world. it's almost like the vibe is set by cinematography, soundtrack, camera placement (all those methodical devices Malick uses) and when characters start talking, even from minimal interactions and dialogue you can still feel emotions oozing, one would think the movie like this would come off dreadfully pretentious, instead what you get is an immersive sublime experience that doesn't hide anything and furthermore invites you to be part of it.

as for the meaning behind it, I'm not sure I did grasp all the hidden symbolism in film, as far as I know this is very personal film for Malick, I read somewhere that Malick's brother died by suicide when he was young which is basically what took place in "The Tree of Life". to me "The Tree of Life" is meditative look and study on anything from creation of life to childhood trauma and family relations and how Interconnected we are with pretty much everything even if we seem to be just a speck of dust in this universe. it might come off bit corny but with the universe creation scene I feel like Malick is saying  "You are the universe" to audience, that notion for me is also reinforced when Jessica Chastain's character has this monologue where she ask "what are we to you" and immediately solar explosion happens and the "creation" commences. the film of course has some depressing moments but it seems to be ultimately optimistic, in the ending Sean Penn walks into afterworld where he meets his mother, father and brothers. the film definitely pushes the idea for forgiveness, reconciling with your past and loving one another (family mainly but not neccesarily) despite all the hardships you're facing, this is also reinforced by quote "only way to be happy is to love". in the end you have a film that's bigger than pretty much anything imo, it's constantly and abundantly loaded with meaningful implicit messages, many of those messages may get lost due to subtlety because The Tree of Life never tries to stress its concepts like most movies, I've never seen existential film like this that touches on basically everything without overstaying it's welcome. this is how I interpret "The Tree of Life" but like I said I'm sure many stuff flew over my head or escaped my attention so I'd like to ask everyone's interpretation of this film, what you guys think about this film and what it's ultimately trying to say in your opinion ?  


this is the best film I've ever laid my eyes on and I'm not sure if I'll see anything better in future, this is also one of those films that I gotta make life-time commitment to see it in theater.



### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Someone earlier made a post here asking why some movies could be slow and it not bother you and I didn’t reply because I don’t know how to put it into words but yeah Tree of Life is like that for me. Terence Malick and Kubrick movies probably seem slow to others but I don’t feel it at all because I’m totally immersed in the movie. 

Everything fades away, I get this strange ineffable feeling. At first I tried to rationalize it as the films feel so rich in subtext my mind is chewing on them during those quiet moments, like all the slow scenes in 2001: A Space Odyssey, but thinking back I’m not actually thinking in those moments, I’m lost, in that perfect way which is the reason I consume media in the first place.

I definitely consider its themes afterwards but mostly I just feel the movie and it’s something only a few directors are really capable of in that same way. Kubrick, Malick, Paul Thomas Anderson with The Master and There Will Be Blood, I don’t know why but that style totally subsumes me.

Also I haven’t seen it in a while but Tree of Life doesn’t particularly strike me as “ultimately” trying to say anything, besides maybe, “Holy shit life is wild huh? Can you believe all this? Dinosaurs? Fathers, death, life, capitalism?” I think it’s more about letting that existential feeling wash over you.
  - I totally agree, Malick films have this laid-back vibe I can't explain it, it's almost like movie reassuring you "it's okay you don't have think about plot, just sit down, relax and enjoy" , that's why they have very few dialogues, it's almost letting your mind have some space to breathe, It's very unconventional way of watching a film but I'll be damned if it's not the best.
  - I grew up watching things like 2001 with my dad, so I was pretty good at going where a film wanted to take me, but it was Tarkovsky who finally cracked me totally in that regard. I remember watching Solaris and losing interest during that opening 10 minutes of kelp or whatever undulating in a pond (felt like 10 minutes), 

I really wanted to watch the movie, but I remember laughing to myself at what a self-indulgent “artiste” Tarkovsky was for that choice. And I thought, “Why *open* a film this way?? Completely alienate and bore your audience!”

But then I really thought about “why open a film this way,” and it occurred to me be was sort of..trying to lull/push us into a certain almost meditative headspace to begin watching his film. And, I can meditate. What was off was my expectation, of what a film was supposed to do.

Tarkovsky didn’t really abide by that. Neither did Kubrick of course, but Tarkovsky, I mean..later in Solaris there’s another 10 minutes just watching someone travel by freeway. 

But my expectations had changed by the time I went to watch it again, and so by the time I got to that scene, I did chuckle, but I settled back and allowed it to just do its thing, I let the movie guide me instead of checking how long until the scene was over or becoming distracted. 

It’s a brilliant, beautiful film. It has several of these moments which demand something of the audience. I get what he was trying to do, and if you can let it, it works. 

Now, there’s basically no long scene I can’t handle. I mean, if the whole thing is really doo doo or it’s not done well, I still have a human reaction. For instance, I do actually think the hour and 40 minute (could be exaggerating but I think this might be close to true) wedding scene in Deer Hunter is excessive and unnecessary. Maybe others feel about it the way I feel about Tarkovsky. 

But for me it’s the silence. The meditation. Forcing you to slow down. I’m into it. I’m glad I let myself get there because it’s brought me a lot, in the way of enjoying and fully experiencing some of my favorite things. 

Since then btw I’ve read Tarkovsky’s book “Sculpting in Time” and it’s even more clear how deliberate this all was, and how essential he felt these sorts of scenes were to bringing the audience into an experience as he imagined it. 
2. I grew up in east Africa as an expat in the 90s, in a suburb strangely like this films Waco, and with parents eerily similar (the dinner table slap, an angelic mother, the dichotomies), wandering around with my brothers, the awe of nature, harsh realities. and for all the reasons stemmming from that, it's also the greatest film I've ever seen
3. It’s in my all-time top 5 movies. I’ve always viewed the creation scene as a preface to the rest of the film, essentially saying: ‘We are going to discuss a lot of heavy topics, but remember that you and your problems don’t matter in the grand scheme of things.’

  - I actually always saw it in an opposite way, it was like Malick affirming us and comforting us that despite the hardships life is beautiful, the creation scene confirms it for me because if something this immense, beautiful and complex emerged out of nowhere and created life as we know it, then there should be a reason for us being here and nothing happens coincidentally. I see this sequence as very affirming and consoling way to express sorrow and offer something Cherishable, hope.
4. It was because you didn’t watch a film telling you a story. You experienced something. The film unfolds in the same way in which I dream and daydream. The same way in which I experience memories. I got lost in an experience where I felt as if I was in a constant state of remembrance of a life lived. My first watch of this film was in a theater and it felt like an out of body experience. No film that I had viewed prior to that had ever attempted something as bold, nor as effective.  
The film is an experience of forces, including love, loss, purpose, determination, grace, etc. I wouldn’t claim that the film is “trying to say” anything at all. Malick wanted to give an experience of a life lived.
  - well said, this reminded me why I enjoyed reading In Search of Lost Time some 15 years after enjoying The Tree of Life
  - They say exactly this on the Criterion "documentary" with Lubezki and the rest of the crew
5. Everyone else here has articulated it so much more beautifully than I could, but I love how you describe your experience.

*The Tree of Life* is one of those rare watches where you just feel rather than disseminate. For me, that's a small list and I cherish those when they come around.

## 2. I found Marty Supreme disappointing

**Score:** 99
**Body:** I was so let down by this movie. I try to avoid knowing anything about movies as much as possible before going to see them. With Marty Supreme that was difficult since everyone was talking about it but I really only wanted to see it because I saw Tyler the Creator was in it. I didn't know it was the same writer and director as Uncut Gems but pretty early on I started to think it was.   
There were similar themes like "success at all costs" and gambling, over confidence that can be unfounded, etc, and I think those are fun themes in films. My biggest complaint for Marty Supreme was how little I felt like I could connect to anyone in this story.  
One of the best things about Uncut Gems is that the characters are both likable and unlikable at the same time. I felt like I cared about them as I watched their lives unfold in front of me. I cried when Howie had his meltdown with his girlfriend not because I thought he was a good guy or deserved redemption but because I know the feeling of trying your best and things not working out and I could feel his desperation. I did not feel anything like that in Marty Supreme. The closest I came to that was the "paddle" scene but it really did not connect the same way for me.  
I think the concept of the character of Marty is great but the execution felt hollow. I read that Safdie wrote backstories for every character in that movie but I didn't feel that shining through at all. Not that the characters felt unrealistic, just that they all felt unrelatable and boring.  
The worst part about this is when I try to find reviews of people who agree with me, all I am finding is people criticizing Marty and calling him a bad guy, unsympathetic and a narcissist. I feel like those people are missing the point of the film. I didn't think it was a bad movie, I just wanted more from it.   
Uncut Gems is one of my favorite movies. There are so many layers to it and it has an almost mystical or spiritual vibe to it along with themes of race, shame, religion, family, loyalty, risk and addiction. I am both rooting for and against Howie the whole movie but there are so many other moving parts happening at the same time! The movie isn't just about Howie. Marty Supreme is just about Marty. Which would be fine if I felt like I could connect with him at all in any way.  
Maybe this has just been my experience but I love to hear other opinions in agreement or disagreement, I would love to discuss, not here to try to shoot the film down. I personally didn't like it but would love to have my mind changed.

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. My take on my dislike of Marty Supreme is that it takes this Mafia mentality of Marty of doing things and finding out without putting a kind of weight to its repercussion or support as if those lies and deception he did would just fly to nowhere. It is not even smart or well planned ruckus. All are accident cascading to him gliding away. I actually argued in my own personal review, that maybe the idea of poverty and maybe its ongoing might be something the Safdies are not familiar. There is a sense of just him trying to run away from responsibility any where he could. 

This on top of the huge ego trip. The use of Jewishness for moral righteousness. His 'career' as the top table tennis  being just him being angry about things not going his way. The two leading ladies treating him like he is the best meat there is all against one dimensional 'antagonist'. A lot of unearned Hero characterization for me. 

It was just not my film. 
  - >without putting a kind of weight to its repercussion or support as if those lies and deception he did would just fly to nowhere.

Is this not what the entire movie is about? None of the schemes he runs ever pans out and even the climax of the movie, the grand exposition it build towards, is ultimately for nothing, because of course it is. He was never getting into that tournament, he was only ever running headlong into the brick wall of reality. His lies and deception finally catch up to him and maybe that (and more importantly his child) will wake him up.
2. I watched Uncut Gems when it originally came out and think both films are roughly the same quality, even if I personally liked Marty Supreme a bit more. What you say about Uncut Gems, that "there are so many layers to it", I think can be said about Marty Supreme too. While "success at all costs" is certainly a major theme, I feel it is a bit simplistic to reduce Marty Supreme just down to that. 

In many ways, Marty Supreme seems to me like it is a multifaceted approach to the American Zeitgeist right after World War II, with all its idiosyncracies and complexities (mind you, this is just what it seemed like to me, I'm not American myself). Marty wants success at all costs, yet at times he also treats table tennis more like a spectacle and a performance art rather than a competition. He is boastful and fills his matches with pageantries and theatrical antics, yet at the same time he really wants to prove himself as the best player in the world. At least to me, this feels like America's self-perception right after World War II: the best country in the world, but one that constantly feels the need to prove this not simply through results, but through an extra layer of idolatry. What was impressive to me is that the film really doesn't seem to just look at this in a cynical way. Marty genuinely loves what he does, he loves both the competition and the spectacle, the sport and the art. His final match with Endo (who is presented both as his equal and yet someone completely different) really nails this I think: a fierce, competitive table tennis match that simultenously feels like a dance or a professional wrestling match, with the crowd constantly at the edge of their seats. He wins, and even though it should really be meaningless, to him it feels incredibly meaningful, not only because he proved how good he was, but also because he got to stand on a stage and engross a crowd of two entirely different cultures with an incredible performance. Him hugging Endo after the match and telling him what a great player he is only furthers how much he loves what he does.

And I do think this is where another layer of Marty Supreme comes in, that it shows us just how much we are often willing to do for the things we love, not purely out of a drive to succeed, but out of a genuine passion. The old gangster loves his dog so much that he is ultimately willing to die for it, while Gwyneth Paltrow's character traded her passion in for a cushy, secure and normative life that only caused her unhappiness. Neither path is presented as perfect and even Marty arguably loses as much as he gains by the end. The film could have well just presented sacrifice for passion as something universally bad, but I feel like that would have not been nearly as effective as the nuanced look it ended up providing. I also think that Marty's happy reaction at seeing his child was a genuine one, because unlike the endless greed and eventually exploitation that a lifestyle like his can lead to (as seen by Rockwell), Marty seems genuinely content with his win over Endo and how they elevated table tennis and is now ready to turn towards a new passion. Will he be a good father? Who knows, but I also don't think it really matters to the film.

I should say that I'm probably a bit biased towards Marty Supreme. I used to play table tennis and quite like watching sports. Hell, I cried during the final table tennis match. But I also think that sports as performance art and the imperfect triumph of passion over greed are core subjects of Marty Supreme that a lof of people miss because they (as you say) wrongfully focus on how "unlikeable" Marty is or, at least I think so, because they compare the film too much with Uncut Gems (which is also a fantastic movie). Besides all of this, I also think Marty Supreme has great editing, a fantastic score and an incredible acting performance by Chalamet, all of which can take the viewer out of the erratic stress of the live of Marty Mauser and into the transcendentalism that many of these seemingly vain attempts at fulfillment actually hold.
  - This is incredibly insightful thank you!
3. I think there is a lot about being Jewish in the aftermath of the holocaust going on in this film that you were not clocking. Race, weakness, religion, identity, oppression, obsession, meaning, power dynamics etc is all in play. This movie is very much not just about Marty imo.
  - You know what that does make sense. I think the scene with the honey was so profoundly disturbing I think it was my favorite part honestly. Made me feel sick. 
    - That scene was based on a true story, as you may know.
      - I actually did not know. Jesus Christ.
  - I agree. Down to the moments where he says he loves his friends, there’s some cultural things going on in that film that really interested me more than just the main story and main character. 
4. There was a lot to like in Marty Supreme - the casting choices and the ensemble cast’s performances, the cinematography, and the fucking soundtrack in the theater. But I do feel the story was a bit too predictable and particularly felt like a Safdie Formula, and Odessa’s character felt a little bit underdeveloped. Honestly even Marty himself felt a bit one dimensional. I enjoyed watching it in the theater, but I am on the fence about whether I would go out of my way to watch it again

## 3. Did Eastwood build the slasher template five years before Halloween? In a Western?

**Score:** 33
**Body:** Had this absolute flash of memory today.

I think there is a line between High Plains Drifter and the Creeper from Jeepers Creepers. I don't know if this is intentional or not, but maybe a very, very odd coincidence.

In High Plains Drifter, Eastwood plays The Stranger. The town of Lago is hiding a dark secret, they watched Marshal Jim Duncan get whipped to death and did nothing. The Stranger arrives like a supernatural force of retribution. But instead of going all guns blazing, he psychologically dismantles the town, gets them to paint it red and rename it Hell, breaks their minds before he breaks their bodies. Eastwood confirmed in interviews that The Stranger is the vengeful ghost of Marshal Duncan, returned to claim what's owed. The film ends with the character Mordecai realizing he's been talking to a dead man, as The Stranger cryptically confirms his identity standing next to Duncan's tombstone.

Now if you look at the visual architecture. Eastwood directed the film and used deliberate low-angle shots to make himself appear as a looming, unstoppable force of nature. Jeepers Creepers uses the exact same camera logic to construct the Creeper. Both figures use the same silhouette, the tall frame, the duster, the hat, concealing their true nature from a distance. In High Plains Drifter, the costume hides the fact that he's a dead man. In Jeepers Creepers, the duster hides wings and monstrous anatomy while the hat obscures a disfigured face. One wears a brown duster and a gun belt, the other wears a tattered green duster. Set side by side, the silhouette is almost identical. They could've taken a fit from any other western. But the Jeepers Creeper looks like The Stranger.

In a deleted scene from the first movie, the Creeper actually speaks after killing the Cat Lady, the Creeper holds her corpse up to Darry and Trish and says "She don't smell too good, Darius..." in an Old Western accent, before tossing her aside.

The Stranger returns because a specific sin was committed and went unpunished. The Creeper returns every 23rd spring for 23 days to feed. One is morally triggered, the other is cosmically scheduled, but both are cyclic returners of punishment arriving on a timer tied to transgression and consumption. Hell is coming to town, and it is always coming back.

Both films also weaponize not knowing what the characters are dealing with. In High Plains Drifter the terror is almost entirely psychological before it turns physical. It's classified as horror, but it's not scary for the viewer, you're not holding your breath, it's scary for the town people in the film. In Jeepers Creepers the entire first act runs on pure dread. You don't know what the Creeper is, you don't know its motives, and almost nothing about its origins is ever revealed.

High Plains Drifter came out in 1973. Halloween was 1978. Friday the 13th was 1980. Eastwood built the unstoppable supernatural punisher archetype, the cyclic returner, the figure of inevitable retribution, the monster that breaks minds before it breaks bodies, five years before the slasher genre formally existed. The lineage never gets traced back to him because the film lives in the western category and the western and horror canons don't talk to each other.

Before 1973, movie killers were mostly human, pieces like Psycho or Peeping Tom. Eastwood introduced a killer who cannot be killed by normal means, does not speak much, well...he doesn't speak much in the Dollar Trilogy either, but you get the point, is enigmatic, and has a symbolic connection to the setting. When Halloween came out in '78, John Carpenter, who's a huge western fan, leaned into that same force of nature vibe.

Then in 2001, Victor Salva makes Jeepers Creepers and literally puts that archetype back into the western costume it came from.

Did Eastwood invent the unstoppable supernatural slasher villain?

Just an observation.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It's interesting that you mention the spaghetti western influence on Eastwood, because Italy also had a proto-slasher film genre called giallo that started in the 60's and because really popular in 1970 will the release of Bird With The Crystal Plummage. 

I'm not sure if Eastwood was familiar with the genre at the time, but you can see some of it's influence in 80's slasher movies. I know for a fact that Friday the 13th Part 2 directly ripped off scenes from A Bay of Blood that came out back in 1971.
2. Interesting writeup, you've convinced me to go watch the film.

However, I don't know if your analysis holds up, specifically here:

>High Plains Drifter came out in 1973. Halloween was 1978. Friday the 13th was 1980. Eastwood built the unstoppable supernatural punisher archetype, the cyclic returner, the figure of inevitable retribution, the monster that breaks minds before it breaks bodies, five years before the slasher genre formally existed

The problem is that "unstoppable supernatural punisher" is an extremely broad and old archetype, and also not a particularly good description of most slasher villains. The pre-70s prototypes (Norman Bates of *Psycho*, Mark Lewis of *Peeping Tom*, and the many *giallo* villains) aren't cyclic punishers - Norman is psychotic and driven by sexual shame, Lewis is a sexual sadist, and most *giallo* villains are either crazy people, sex killers, or criminals out for profit. Moving into the 70s, we have Billy from *Black Christmas*, who is just some anonymous psycho who decides to kill a bunch of young women in a sorority completely arbitrarily. We also have Leatherface from *The Texas Chain Saw Massacre*, who is a mentally disabled man who kills on behalf of his cannibal chef older brother, who likewise targets the protagonists simply because they happen to be in the area. Then finally we come to Michael Myers of *Halloween*, who has the cyclic element in the sense that he only kills on Halloween night, but in the first film there's no punishment or retribution aspect, as Michael targets Laurie and her friends for no particular reason.

In the 80s, we have *Friday the 13th*, but that movie doesn't feature a cyclic, supernatural punisher, it turns out that the killer is a traumatized, completely human woman killing for the first time. We do have several cyclic retribution type killers in the early 80s (*The Prowler, The Burning,* and Jason in the *Friday* sequels), but none of those are supernatural.

The first major example of an explicitly supernatural killer I can think of is Freddy Krueger of *A Nightmare on Elm Street*, but Freddy's murders are only retributive in an incidental sense - he was already a child killer in life, it just so happens that when he returns as a spirit that he can both resume killing kids for the fun of it AND get revenge on the people who killed him, as a bonus.

I think the first film that actually fits all elements (supernatural, cyclic, kills for revenge) of your description of a typical slasher villain is *Friday the 13th Part VI: Jason Lives* in the mid-80s. There are other later examples, but I can't think of any earlier ones, and so I think that your impression of what a typical slasher villain is like doesn't really match the heyday of the genre, you're just thinking of the latter period Jason.

I think you might have a point about a connection between *High Plains Drifter* and *Jeepers Creepers,* specifically, but the whole idea of the supernatural force of revenge is an ancient trope, and as shown above it's not even a trope that is specifically associated with the slasher genre.
3. Interesting. I remember John Carpenter said he turned down Fatal Attraction because he felt it was a blatant ripoff of Play Misty For Me. I wonder if Carpenter would've went ahead had he known how massive it'd be?
4. As with so many things, I believe you look at these works along a continuum.

I’ve long felt that *High Plains Drifter* took the Sergio Leone/Eastwood “Man With No Name” concept (I know, I know… he had an actual name in each film but still!) and moved it a little more into the area of the supernatural.  But it was established -I felt- in the Dollars trilogy that Eastwood’s character tended to be *almost* supernatural.  Yes, he could be hurt and hurt quite badly but there are moments where he seems to almost supernaturally move from place to place without villains knowing where he’s at and takes them out almost as if he can walk through walls or is near invisible…!

Interestingly -and I don’t know if he was being facetious- but if memory serves Eastwood was asked about his character and whether he was a spirit (I think it’s pretty obvious he is a vengeful spirit) and he said something to the effect that he was the Sheriff’s brother… perhaps.

What makes HPD such an interesting work is that it takes the “spaghetti” western concept and does indeed move it into the supernatural realm.  Eastwood’s mysterious stranger is indeed a vengeful spirit/demon whose goal is a mystery for most of the film.  While his character isn’t really a “slasher” villain (he does kill, but it isn’t this super gory stuff that would come later on) he is very much a borderline villain rather than an “anti-hero” as some of his other characters were.

Someone also pointed out that the plot of HPD is essentially like that of *High Noon*.  Think about that… You get villains on their way to town and the town is all jittery about their return.  They go to the “Sheriff” for help and, unlike *High Noon*, he does get the townspeople -who are indeed cowards- to help him out in taking the incoming villains.

But then, in a turn from *High Noon*, he walks away and leaves them to their fate.  In *High Noon*, the Sheriff of course takes them on almost single-handedly.  In HPD, he -perversely- lets the townspeople face their fate!
5. Interesting connection. Some googling definitely points to The Golem (1915) as being the first movie with a supernatural slasher monster but I guess it depends on how you define it.
6. I think one key difference is that the protagonist of High Plains Drifter is out for some old testament style of justice. He wasn't a "killer" in the horror sense of the term because he was basically an avenging angel. The slasher antagonists are evil, they attack largely indiscriminately. Michael Myers isn't a punisher, he's evil incarnate whose only purpose is to kill. Also from my understanding the aforementioned Psycho and Peeping Tom ARE considered the first slasher films, although Halloween obviously popularized them and evolved them into something else.

## 4. Why do some slow movies work better than others?

**Score:** 26
**Body:** I’ve been thinking about slow-paced films and why some feel deeply emotional while others just feel boring.

For me, it works when the slower scenes reveal something about the character or mood. But when nothing really changes, it can feel like the film is just stretching time.

What makes a slow film work for you? Is it atmosphere, character detail, cinematography, or something else?

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I got one for you, that might change your view on this topic: Sound of Falling. 

It's a German film from 2025 and it's so slow that watching it is torturing, at least for me. I tried to replicate the dialogue speed in real life and my wife got angry at me immediately.

But if you manage to get along with it, it makes something with you. I don't know, if it "worked" because I don't know if that film is intentionally annoying it's viewers, but it certainly increases pain tolerance because it doesn't compromise on slowness.
  - Oh my God finally someone mentioning that thing, its the first thing it came to my mind when i read this, even tho we have different experiences. I hated it and was on the verge of getting out of the movie theater when watching it. It was a torture. Similar to La chimera. I said it on similar threads weeks ago, those two films look and sound to me like someone who theoretically knows how to make slow and art cinema decided to make film on its own, but it completly lacks the soul.

And im not saying that as someone who cant bear with slow cinema - that subgenre is my very favourite, and Tarkovsky Ozu and Antonioni (who is also sometimes accused of being glacial) are my personal favourites. Their films have something that contemporary ones absolutely do not (in my opinion), even tho on the surface they are all "slow".
2. Watching Bela Tarr made me realise how important framing and blocking are. If those are done perfectly, for me it doesn't really matter what's actually happening on the screen.

And therein lies the mystery of the cinematic experience and its unique hypnotic quality.
  - Satantango's hefty 7 hours never felt long. However, I "cheated" and watched it in three installments (still 2+ hours per leg). It drew me in. Can't really explain it. Everything moves exceptionally slow, but nonetheless, I was hooked. Haven't seen any of his other works, yet.

(not really contributing to this discussion, just wanted to chime in)
3. Principally, cinematography.

Tarkovsky films wouldn't work for me if they weren't formally beautiful. I couldn't be entranced by Nikolaus Geyrhalter's documentary [*Homo sapiens*](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2rDb5Satizw) if there wasn't something to observe in every corner of the frame.
4. Generally, a script for any good film is very tight and cut to the bone. However, the cinematography and pacing are also crafted to help the viewer actually digest the beats and conversations in the movie, especially in slower, low-impact, slow-burning films. 

When you think about it, it works a bit more like a painting in a museum or a piece of art. It's there for the viewer to make their own interpretations about what the piece is and what it means.

Keep in mind, however, that a film is literally a directed experience. All things in life are less about content and more about execution. Can the director, through a film, place you in that movie to the point that you are there, emotionally and mentally engaged? 

Now, the flaw of any creator is that they are blinded by their own work. Or led to points where reality does not meet the vision, and compromises have to be made. This is even before creators who don't know what they are doing.   

It's why a treatment of a script is generally pages and pages longer than the actual script, before things are cut down. A scene might have ten lines of dialogue in the final script, but the actual treatment is several pages long for that scene.  

Some works never reach that spot, or things fall apart when actually filming it. Like, I know tons of script making, but I couldn't tell you the first steps in how to actually start filming a slow film.
5. It's never been directly about the length for me, it comes down to the pacing for sure. Some movies are slow as hell and don't even reveal much about the characters, and then you have movies like eeaao, where it's quite short for the type of movie it is, but still is able to show great emotion in the characters in scenes that can range from short and minimal to a bit longer
6. I think *Nomadland*, while not my favorite slow movie, did a great job demonstrating the effectiveness of a slow movie as an externalized character study. You really understand at the end the choice she makes >!not to stay!<, not because you’d make the same choice, but because you’ve seen things from her perspective so much that words don’t have to be said.

I will say that *Tree of Life* did not do it for me, and I have not spend enough time with Malick beyond *Thin Red Line* to know if I like his style. I think it’s too dreamy for me, and not enough settling in a dialogue or scene. *Assassination of Jesse James* was great, and surely counts.

These contemplative movies are very different from slowburn movies, like *2001*, *There Will Be Blood*, or *The VVitch* (or *Mother!* if you like that sort of thing). Suspense is really not the point of a slow movie, and can’t sustain the viewer’s attention. Instead, I usually get more pulled in by an interest in what the director is saying metaphorically. Perhaps I did not get hypnotized from *Tree of Life* for that reason, as the themes seemed pretty much apparent from the beginning rather than unfolding to some extent.
7. Because what they show you is interesting. Like tarkovsky is slow but incredibly beautiful. On the other hand, somes films  copy the slowness but show you something mundane and boring.
8. This is something I've been thinking about a lot as I've accidentally watched a fair few films that would be classified under 'slow cinema' this year - Stalker, Sangatsu no Raion, The Mastermind at home; The Stranger in the Fog, Magellan, Blow-Up, The Passenger, Au Hasard Balthasar in the cinema. 

I struggled with all the films I watched at home to some degree or another, but I didn't universally love the films I saw in the cinema either. Stalker is an undisputed classic and I think I would've enjoyed it much more in the cinema - the benefit of a slow-paced film is highly dependent on the viewer. Slow films force you to sit with your thoughts instead of being consistently entertained / distracted.

I loved Magellan, for example, and I wouldn't consider the minimalistic style and pace of the film to dive into psychologically complex characters - instead, I found the emotion of the film much more powerful and compelling because I was forced to sit quietly and observe man's casual inhumanity to man. There's not much 'plot' and the characters are not very expressive or interesting, but it is saying something important about history and about evil. 

Blow-Up or The Passenger, on the other hand, have very interesting stories and characters, and the sedate pacing helped me feel more immersed. With the films I didn't like, I felt there was nothing interesting worth sitting with, and so the slow moments became dull, sometimes painfully so. 

Lav Diaz is a good example since Magellan is in colour and has some really beautiful cinematography at sea, whereas the only other film of his I'd seen, When the Waves are Gone, is in black and white and fairly low-budget. The most memorable scenes of that film were shot very simply, and I struggled with it much more when I saw it - I hated it at the time, but it stuck in my mind. That was my first time watching real 'slow cinema' and I struggled to stay awake in the cinema, finding it all a bit painful. Becoming more comfortable with the idea that being bored or sleepy is a legitimate reaction to the art, not a failing of the viewer, has helped me appreciate it, and films like it, more. If I went back and rewatched When the Waves Are Gone I'd still probably not love it, but I would have had a better experience.

Since the viewer's response and how they reflect on the film while watching it is so personal and subjective I guess slow-paced films can be judged by how good they are at inspiring that quality, but that's still very hard to quantify. By the time I'd watched Au Hasard Balthasar in the cinema I'd seen all the other films I mentioned so I thought I knew what I was getting into, but I was hardly moved at all - perhaps because I grew up Catholic and find Bresson's exploration of Catholic themes deeply uninteresting, so I had little to reflect on. Sangatsu no Raion had some nice gritty 90s Kawasaki scenery but the story and characters evoked less than nothing from me, but the reviews on letterboxd made it sound like a film I would love. 

I've said all this to conclude with "idk man, the vibes?" Hopefully someone who's seen more slow cinema can chime in, because I do tend to find these films interesting and want to know how to analyse them better myself.
